speaker: Alben Halen
words: That's not for a kid. So I didn't dig that much more than four feet. But I dug graves down there. We had to get them down to six feet. And you know they've changed all that stuff. They never dig a grave that deep anymore. Dig them about five feet. When they get that cement blocks around, there's a bird's coffin in there. I don't think they're more than about that far from top. Down to the... Having... Somebody dug a grave up there. Six feet deep. Come to bury the guy while they didn't have them stripes. Then they got nothing to... let them down. They wasn't half long enough so they had to go and get a bunch of ropes and stuff to put it down.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No, they tried to get by as easy as they can I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when do you start hitting the clay?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Pretty bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do you start hitting the clay pretty bad? Right close to the surface?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well, for the first two feet you could get down pretty good. And after that why... It got harder and harder.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No, I was worked on lots of that grave digging. Somebody died while I was asked somebody to go and dig. Probably five, six men would go and dig that grave. There was no charge on anything like that. Nobody ever charged for it. Nowadays why it's different. You get one down the back hose and dig it out. I guess you pay off from twenty -five to fifty dollars for a grave.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it take a very long time to dig in that clay?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No, it didn't take too awful long. You know you can't work more than in a grave. Two men can work in there of course. One each end and each end. And you work like heck when you're down in there but you don't let it stay there too long until you tire out or anything. Somebody else will take it.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: That is up over on this other cemetery there. We had one awful time. A guy died. Arnold Orvig died and we were going to dig a grave. So we got up to start digging and all the ones that got in the head, they were going to shoot it. Shoot it. No, let's not shoot it. Oh yes, we'll shoot it. So much easier digging. So they put in a charge of dynamite, dug down to loosen up the dirt. All they got that done, the other grave, made a year or two right up the side of the top.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: The wet and everything but water and that's all just from the other grave and then to what they were digging. They had one time they had planks and everything else and I don't know how they ever got it out. Dig water

speaker: Alben Halen
words: and you know what it smells like coming from another grave.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: The way to dig them nowadays is what I can dig in harder ground. Well it wasn't harder when we could dig it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well which cemetery was that?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: That wasn't the Dry Creek. No, that wasn't Dry Creek. It's up here where the church is standing right out of town on the south side where he was buried.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: The biggest grave I ever helped to dig was

speaker: Alben Halen
words: room enough to set tree coffins down at the same time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was that?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well a car wreck and killed Tom Weibach and Mrs. Weibach and one daughter and we buried her down on Barridge and it was no use to drive to space and we took the whole works and a lot of men there we dug it so they just set the coffins down down in there. That's the biggest grave I ever helped to dig. Dig a cistern or something.

words: I think it was I'm sure my wife would know the name. Yeah I've seen it in some place in writing but I don't remember that's okay.

words: Well they probably went to school with them. No no no they went more, it would be more like going to school with Mary than it would me my daughter. But one fell off the raft and the other one went in to try to get her Well one fell off probably and the other one probably was the raft dipped over some and throwed that one off.

words: No not quite.

words: Yeah she said yeah. Well he was here for quite a few years I guess.

words: Yeah they was the ones that started it.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Sure there was nothing but the Potlatch concerned the whole thing. As far as Bovill goes up there was nothing but a Potlatch man. He was seeing that they got all that land filed for and so they could get in there and buy the timber.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And Bill Helmer I guess he was a locator. He took the lines out and anybody wanted a timber clean but he'd take them to another place. That's the way that worked. Hugh Henry they wanted to put the railroad in so they could log their logs to Potlatch. Well they logged. They had that mail of clues to start with.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No Bovill didn't live there up there for nothing. Heck yeah that's the place if anybody wanted to come you know wanted to be located and work and like the mother forest going out there in Michigan land they had to have some place to live. Of course they put up the hay on the meadows and fell tree barns there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they'd stay at the Bovill Hotel?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah. No I don't think Bovill built that hotel. There was an hotel there but I don't believe it's the same one that he lived in. I wish my sister would have been alive while she could have told you. She worked there for seven years straight for Bovill. Waiting table and doing up beds there and help with the cooking. So you know she knew every son of a gun that come up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she think of the place?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I heard it was real pretty there before they come in. Oh it was nice. It was real nice. All that tall big timber standing up around them meadows and that meadows was just happy with grass. Grass listed up this high. That's the way all the meadows was up in here. White meadows and shea meadows and how it works. Open spot, grass no timber on it. Up there at hog meadows You know where that's at. Well that's the same way there.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Let's see what was his name.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I can't Well he was an old wrestler. Thought he was anyway. He lived there. Donner. Donner that sounds right. Donner I knew him well. First old man went and the boys worked in the woods and one got crippled up pretty bad.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Two as far as that goes. Donner's mad I had no idea. He farmed some out in trash green up there. The rest was in hay.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: But I don't know how many has had it after that.

words: Well most of those meadows, most of them were just used for grazing weren't they?

words: I mean most of them weren't cleared up and grown crops on in the early years. Well the early years the grass just grew up there. And they cut out the mower and they'd have slough grass up like that. And just the tick of the hair on the dog's back. I was with Nick and Joe up there supposed to drive parking. Of course I shoveled some hay too but on the young punk of a kid. Him and old Ross Lawrence put a pay up on them meadows.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And it looked nice in there then timber wasn't taken out. Well it was nice here too if you wanted to call it nice when the timber was all standing. Like that field over there, there was no field. There was no field here. Big nice smooth yellow pines.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: 30 inches and 2 feet and bigger on the stump. Oh I remember when there was logging on that mostly on that slope.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Nick and Joe was logging a lot of ground up there. But I can't think of the name of the guy that loved being here.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He was a big, big logger had a camp in here and everything but I can't think of his name.

words: Did Joe do a lot of logging?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Oh yeah he logged here and there wherever. If he could get a contract you know and had a couple of teams and put in a lot of logs.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well he had to do something to make a living. He didn't farm enough there to make 11.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well not a farm land up there anyway. You can't raise anything to any good crop. The priest is too quick up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where his place was?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well all of that land up in there. Borrowed none of it.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I seen any too hot here I'm telling you. I was looking for frost all last summer as far as that goes.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: It's a lot of different from here and just out five, four and a half miles out on the ridge on the weather you know. Out there I used to have floods standing there till the snow flying and they wouldn't freeze down. And up here you know they froze down last fall and the year before but it was getting dry up anyway when it froze down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much longer would you say you get for growing up there? Another two weeks maybe or more? Down on the ridge?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah. Yeah longer than that sometimes.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well right from our house is that why it took quite a way late in the fall before it froze down. Cucumbers and tomatoes and all that stuff that's pretty easy. They'll stand there quite a spell. Oh I raised ripe tomatoes here as far as that goes. I had two plants last year. And the year before I had a dozen big plants.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yes the pot's good to start ripe enough to start picking and I did. A bunch of deer come in and clean them out. Must have been seven deer as they jumped over the bank and what they didn't eat off or they knocked off you know. That's them lay out. They come back afterwards and clean them up anyway what is left. And she gave her and Jim's mother lived in that house. She gave her four plants to plant between the buildings there. She was going and she come home and I told her on Sunday evening you better pick them ripest ones and they'll ripen. Pick them off and they'll ripen. I said I'll do that tomorrow. Tomorrow come she had four big ripe tomatoes hanging there. Deer come and clean them up. Not that crazy for stuff like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you have deer out on the ridge too?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah worse than here but they never come up much to my garden but they was in the orchard all the time. I had a big orchard. Orchard out there. It wasn't so big because I kept cutting it down, getting it down. Must have been 25, 30 trees when I left. That's trees I never for two acres there at one time. Apple trees, prune trees, pear trees.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Sold a lot of fruit to people, company bought. Lot of stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How good were the crops from the orchard? Were they dependable? Could you count on a good fruit yield?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Oh yeah. When the trees was young there was awful nice fruit.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Dad sold 250 boxes of apples to Potts Lus Lumber Company in the fall. I think they sold about 200 boxes later than that in the winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Prunes that come out there at what's the name of the guy before Naugle?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Jones. Jones. He come out to the farm and looked over everything he says. He take everything that's there. But we didn't pick all them anyway but picked them in boxes and everything and come up there, stuck the boxes and went dumping them in an open truck and I drove to all the camps. 2300 pounds of prunes. Lot of prunes.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: It's quite a difference down here. Oh the prunes here are alright. I have prunes on that tree there. Both of them trees and I got one standing there. One up at Little House there. They're not taken care of the way they should be. Out there on the farm you work that ground every year just like an ordinary garden. Every year you heard it and kept moisture in it.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: So they didn't fool with it. It's a lot of work if you're going to raise it. Like here they stand out in the all day one tree there that's worked around because it's where our flower garden and our flower garden come up against the field and I knocked the bank down there and worked up the ground and I worked the ground ever since. They have nice prunes on it this year. And last year too as far as that goes.

words: had slaves and Joe Wells his father was working for for the Wells Frank Crowns was. They took Joe with up here and he took his homestead up here see. That's how he happened to come there. He was a married man and them other two guys he was one married they moved down there to Moscow, out of Moscow where a couple of miles about a trip between the road and the railroad. They had a house in there and lived there for years. Frank died and he was left alone there but he lived quite a few years after that. No they had a blacksmith shop there everybody Frank Wells was a horse doctor got a sick horse go to Frank Wells.

words: And before they come here you know Frank was telling me and one time and me and dad was up there doing some blacksmith work for us he says he had a race horse and him and Joe Wells they slept with the horse all night but he says we were drinking and the next day when the horse got out the horse the horse again $1700 that was offered that for the horse and the horse got out and he wasn't able to do a darn thing. Somebody doped the horse for him that was broke.

words: This was Frank and Joe? Yeah there was boys together at that time see in Krom and the old and I guess they had the horse had the horse and the dad I guess I don't say he owned it but they said we lost the horse and money besides I guess I did so they broke even then they came out there but Frank was no fool on horses he had to ask Dad Colt or anything why Frank Wells to castrate him they all went up there if there was a sick horse he'd give them some remedy or something he'd give them anyway, he tried damn days there wasn't much for medicine anyway but he was nobody's fool Dad bought a horse that had a white eye Dad says he's blind in it he says he's not blind he had some stuff there he went and blotted it like dust and he went and blotted it in his eye imagine that eye cleared up and got like good and asked Dad what it was he said I don't know, didn't ask him it looked like blue stone or sharp but Dad got something to blow into his eye a few times and the eye cleared up otherwise it was just plump white he said it looked like blue stone?

words: Dad didn't know blue stone, that's what they used to treat grain with years and years ago

words: wheat, so it wouldn't smut diluted in water, so much water in the barrel and dipped the sack into it that's the only thing they had for years treating grain but after that I don't know how many things familiar hide

words: and Lawrence's wife had the store and they all pulled out he sat there a long time but he finally took and went down to the store and got a pair of overhauls all the dorms just got a laugh out of it

words: well it happened to him too, I guess he happened to have he would have got somebody to go but they wanted to tell him the nigger anyway well, old Joe, he tore the pants off of him and he had to go and buy another pair and he had nobody to go get him he had to go to the store himself to get him

words: I heard that Joe could have made pretty good money if he didn't drink so bad and spend it all that way.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He made pretty good money on logging he logged all that from the highway and up for that, made with that the winter of 1914 when they fell a bunch of snow out there that they never seen their likes, out on the ranch laid deep in the fence post and then it come a rain on that and it got so hard that they logged on it they took and skidded logs right on top of the snow he logged that whole side hill way up there, big nice yellow pines and took it down to the spur, railroad spur that I put down Pine Creek and I was for a while there, you couldn't turn the horses out, no fences they didn't see a fence cows, you'd have to walk on it, the cows wasn't bad they had the snow tramp down and had to go to water by the pond, they went and drank and they followed their trail, but the horses, if they got out after it went down so I could see the wire fence, I turned the one on one out they'd run on that snow and roll on it kick up the hills and they wouldn't make a mark in the darn stuff well I at that time I was a pretty good sized kid anyway I got my brother when he came home on Sunday and Saturday to go with me out there on a strip of ground that the dad had broke out and trees just standing he just left the big ones big time marks on the tree course we'd dig out all the snow and throw it away and then we'd sew them right down to the ground close to the ground as we could fell down four trees, three, four trees I figured I'd enough for another week and it worked so nice that I laid right up on that snow I was bucking and I shoved that blade down there and worked until I got it and that's to keep the balance in there the saw wouldn't wobble I could just shoot them off they sent me two, three, two cords broken and split and I put that snow on, that wasn't all when I was done I think I had if I remember I had 39 cords, that was better I dug a hole in the snow that I could start to pile on the bare ground and I banked it all out in one pile and I had to snow that deep I had to start in piling here so to get in the pile started and after it got up I could put it up nine feet and stand on the sled driving there and go right along side of it with the horses along that bank in the wooden cave so you know it was some snow how much would you be hauling in one load you think? in the town, I'd have to lay over summer and then I'd haul it next winter I generally haul two cords to the load and that over them hills that was plenty of a load but you know I kid you, I never got enough on them anyway well who was buying the wood then? buying the wood, up along the warehouse here that's the side all around that spur was wood piles, piled up eight feet high some were double piled because they didn't have room they shipped that out and they got a big price, four dollars four dollars, I guess they got a little better, it start with their why, I don't think they got over four and a half of a tamarack a cord, but I think that got on the last wood that I made there I think they got seven dollars a cord but you know it wages was nothing what were your brothers making when they was working for Joe? did they make much money? they got two dollars a day did they stay with him and Lou? they had the bunkhouse, they boarded up when they logged down there that's why they had a cook house and they had a cook in that where Frank and Crumwell used to live they had moved out, Mary cooked there and the daughter, and they had the bunkhouse out there built up for the men to sleep in how many guys was working for him anyway he had a real crew there huh?
